title: Kashmir heatwave prompts school closure, early summer break as soaring mercury grips valley, touches 35.5°C - Mint
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Assam
full_text:
Kashmir heatwave prompts school closure, early summer break as soaring mercury grips valley, touches 35.5°C Kashmir Valley schools will close for summer vacation for two weeks due to heatwave conditions. The state Education Minister Sakina Itoo advised students to take necessary precautions against the heat. Heatwave conditions in Kashmir prompted the Directorate of School Education Kashmir (DSEK) to announce summer vacation for all government and recognised private schools. Schools across the Kashmir Valley will remain closed from 6 July to 19 July, as it witnessed one of the hottest months in over two decades. The region braces for spike in mercury and another heatwave after few days of downpour. Schools up to the higher secondary level will remain closed during the notified period, according to an official order. Classes will resume from 20 July after the vacation in the wake of rise in temperatures across Kashmir over the past several days. This early summer break comes after most arid June since 2014. India received nearly 40% less rainfall than normal last month, recording driest June in 12 years. With 40% less rainfall than normal, it was the fifth-driest month since 1901. "Consequent upon the approval conveyed by the competent authority, it is hereby ordered that all the government and recognised private schools up to higher secondary level functioning in the Kashmir division shall observe summer vacation w.e.f 06.07.2026 to 19.07.2026," DSEK's notification said. IMD director general Mrutyunjay Mohapatra on Tuesday said precipitation this month is expected to be less than 94% of the long-term average of about 28 centimeters, attributing it to the emergence of an El Niño weather pattern in the Pacific Ocean. Jammu and Kashmir Education Minister Sakina Itoo took to social media platform X to advise students to take necessary precautions against the heat and stay safe during the vacation by remaining hydrated. The post stated, "In view of the prevailing weather conditions across the Kashmir Valley, all Government and recognised private schools shall observe Summer Vacation from 6th July to 19th July 2026. Schools will reopen on 20th July 2026." Extending wishes ahead of summer vacation, she added, “The well-being and safety of our students remain our highest priority. I wish all our students, teachers, and school staff a safe, healthy, and refreshing break. I also urge everyone to stay hydrated, take necessary precautions during the heat, and utilise this time for rest, reading, and meaningful family engagement.” As per the India Meteorological Department (IMD), the maximum and minimum daytime temperatures remained 5–6°C and 4-6℃ above normal across most parts of Kashmir, indicating persistent heatwave-like conditions. In the Jammu division, maximum and minimum temperatures were 0–2°C and 1-4℃ above normal at many places. Srinagar recorded one of the highest maximum temperature in the Kashmir division at 35.2°C, while Jammu city was the hottest in the Jammu division with a maximum temperature of 36.3°C. In Kashmir's Konibal, the maximum temperature spiked as high as 35.5°C and in Kupwara it hovered touched 35.4°C. 5-day weather forecast for Jammu and Kashmir Predicting possibility of heavy rains, flash floods, thunderstorm, lightening and gusty winds till 5 July, the IMD issued yellow alert for Kashmir and orange for Jammu division. Monsoon set to cover most of Northwest India According to the India Meteorological Department (IMD), prevailing atmospheric conditions indicate that the Southwest Monsoon has further advanced into Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh, Uttar Pradesh, Uttarakhand, Himachal Pradesh, Ladakh, Jammu and Kashmir, Haryana and Punjab today. Fareha Naaz is a Delhi-based journalist and Content Producer at LiveMint, where she has built nearly three years of experience in digital journalism. Her newsroom experience helped her in combining her critical thinking skills with real-time editorial decision-making. Over the years, she has been presenting complex stories with clarity for a digital-first audience amid fast-paced news cycles. Her thoroughly researched stories, with well-structured and engaging content, provide readers with clear understanding of the context and background.

title: India's June power consumption rises 11.6% on heatwave, delayed monsoon - The Economic Times
search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat" OR heat OR "weather forecast" OR "weather update" OR "weather alert" OR "IMD forecast" OR "IMD prediction" OR "IMD alert" OR IMD OR "rising temperatures" OR "temperatures expected to rise" OR "relief from heat" OR "respite from heat" OR "heat respite" OR "respite from scorching heat") Assam
full_text:
New Delhi: India's power consumption grew by 11.62 per cent to 166.46 billion units (BU) in June compared to the same month last year due to heat wave conditions and late onset of monsoon across the country, which pushed usage of cooling appliances like air conditioners, according to official data.
The country's total power consumption was 149.13 BU in June 2025.

The peak power demand also jumped to 264.76 GW last month from 242. 77GW in June 2025.
The southwest monsoon had set over Kerala on June 4, 2026. Generally, the monsoon arrives in Kerala around June 1, which marks the beginning of the southwest monsoon season (June-September).
Thereafter, the slow progress of the monsoon in June led to heat wave conditions, which increased the use of cooling appliances like air conditioners and desert coolers, thereby increasing power demand and consumption, the experts said.
They are of the view that power demand will be steady in July, as the Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) forecasts a slight rainfall deficit.
The peak power demand had jumped to an all-time record high of 270.82 GW in May, 2025, compared to 230.99 GW recorded in the same month in 2025.
The peak power demand remained at record levels for four days in a row in May this year -- hitting a high of 257.37 GW on May 18, 260.45 GW on May 19, 265.44 GW on May 20 and 270.82 GW on May 21.

The peak power demand was at a record high of 256.11 GW on April 25. Earlier, the peak power demand had touched an all-time high of about 250 GW in May 2024.
According to the power ministry, peak power demand was projected to hit 270 GW in the summer of 2026.
Last summer, the peak power demand was 242.77 GW, recorded in June 2025, but stayed below the government's estimate of 277 GW.
Experts said that power demand and consumption will be steady, as the India Meteorological Department (IMD) has projected a harsh summer this year.

title: What to know about symptoms and treatment for dehydration and heat exhaustion - Inquirer.com
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
full_text:
Brace yourselves, Philadelphians. It’s going to be a hot one out there.
Staying healthy during the heat wave comes down to two basic things: drinking water and cooling down as much as possible.
Here is what you need to know about heat-related illnesses:
What are the signs of dehydration?
Water serves critical functions in the body, including cooling it down, maintaining blood volume, and balancing electrolytes.
Dehydration happens when individuals lose more water than they are taking in. Even though it could happen to anyone, dehydration poses a specific risk to children, elderly, and people with chronic illnesses.
The signs of dehydration are dark-colored urine, less frequent urination, fatigue, confusion, and dizziness. With babies, parents should monitor diapers to ensure that they are continuing to provide urine.
Untreated dehydration can contribute to heat exhaustion or heatstroke, reduced blood pressure, fainting, and seizures.
What are the symptoms of heat exhaustion?
As the body remains overheated, it will continue to sweat and further lose liquids. If individuals’ skin becomes cold and pale, they complain of dizziness and headaches, and seem tired or weak, these are all signs they might be suffering from heat exhaustion.
At this stage, consider calling 911 if a person is vomiting, the symptoms get worse, or last longer than one hour.
What are the symptoms of heatstroke?
Heatstroke is when the body can no longer regulate its temperature. People maystop sweating and spike a fever. The cold, pale skin could turn hot and red. In addition to the fever, people may be confused, pass out, and vomit.
If someone is vomiting, unable to drink, or losing consciousness, medical attention is likely needed in an emergency department. Medical staff there can cool the person down and provide intravenous fluids. If you suspect that someone is suffering heatstroke, call 911.
How to prevent and treat dehydration, heat exhaustion, and heatstroke
Water is your best friend during the heat wave. As much as possible, keep hydrated. Adequate water intake for adults is about 11 to 15 cups a day. While water is not the only hydrating drink, avoid caffeinated and alcoholic drinks, which can contribute to dehydration.
Cooling down is also critical. Look for shade, avoid the sun, and when possible, stay inside air-conditioned buildings. This will help the body regulate heat and preserve liquid, especially during the hottest hours of the day in the afternoon.
There are open splash parks and pools throughout the city where people can go to cool down, though if you spend time in the sun, be sure to put on sunscreen to avoid sunburns.
For people who are dehydrated, get them to a shaded and cooler area and have them drink water. If at any point they lose consciousness, vomit, or are unable to drink, seek medical attention immediately.
How to treat heat rash and cramps
Two other potential unpleasant outcomes of heat are rashes and cramps.
When sweat is trapped in the skin, a potentially itchy heat rash can appear. It can be in the form of small blisters to larger lumps. Heat rashes usually resolve without treatment when the body cools down. The CDC recommends keeping the rash dry and using baby powder to soothe itchiness. Go see a healthcare provider if the rash doesn’t go away within a few days, gets worse, or if you develop additional symptoms or are concerned that other health issues are involved.
Heat cramps are involuntary muscle spasms that can occur due to fluid and electrolyte loss, which iscommon when exercising on a hot day. If you have heat cramps, stop any physical activity, move to a cool place, and drink water. A sports drink with electrolytes can also help.
Seek medical attention if the heat cramps last longer than an hour or if you have a heart condition.

title: As temperatures continue to rise, take precautions to prevent heat stroke - The Victoria Advocate
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
full_text:
As temperatures continue to rise, take precautions to prevent heat stroke
Published 7:00 pm Tuesday, June 30, 2026
As summer temperatures continue climbing across the Crossroads, health officials are warning residents to stay vigilant against heat-related illnesses, especially heat stroke, one of the most dangerous conditions linked to prolonged exposure to extreme heat.
The American Red Cross and the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention provide resources to help residents recognize the signs of heat stroke and prevent heat-related illnesses before they become life-threatening.
With temperatures regularly pushing into the 90s and heat indexes climbing even higher, the risks increase quickly. On Tuesday, Victoria saw a high of 92 degrees, with the heat index reaching 102 degrees.
The CDC outlines several heat-related illnesses, ranging from the less severe heat rash to the most dangerous — heat stroke. Other conditions include heat exhaustion, rhabdomyolysis, heat syncope and heat cramps.
“Heat stroke is the most serious heat-related illness,” said a CDC official. “It occurs when the body can no longer control its temperature: the body’s temperature rises rapidly, the sweating mechanism fails, and the body is unable to cool down. When heat stroke occurs, the body temperature can rise to 106°F or higher within 10 to 15 minutes.”
The Victoria Office of Emergency Management (VOEM) said heat stroke is a life-threatening emergency that can lead to permanent disability or death if left untreated.
Email newsletter signup
Loading ...
VOEM also noted that heat exhaustion is often the body’s warning sign that heat stroke could be next. On social media, the agency shared several safety reminders to help residents stay safe during extreme heat, including:
Both the Red Cross and CDC recommend seeking medical attention immediately if someone shows symptoms of heat stroke or heat exhaustion. The agencies stress that removing the person from the heat as quickly as possible can make all the difference.
According to the CDC, symptoms of heat stroke include confusion, altered mental status, slurred speech, loss of consciousness, hot dry skin or profuse sweating, seizures and a very high body temperature.
To treat heat stroke, the CDC recommends calling 911 immediately, staying with the person, moving them into a cool or shaded area and cooling them rapidly using cold water or ice baths if available. If ice baths are not possible, wet the skin with cool water or place cold wet cloths on the head, neck, armpits and groin.
Heat exhaustion, often caused by excessive sweating and the loss of water and salt, can be treated by moving the person out of the heat and encouraging small sips of cool water. Removing unnecessary clothing, including shoes and socks, and cooling the head, face and neck with cold water can also help.
Humans are not the only ones vulnerable in extreme heat. Pets, especially dogs, can also suffer heat-related illnesses that can become fatal if left untreated.
PetMD, a resource for veterinarians and pet owners, says pet heat stroke can quickly escalate.
“Heatstroke in dogs overpowers the body’s defense mechanisms by damaging heat shock proteins, which are designed to protect the body from stress and heat,” PetMD said. “If the (pet) continues to be exposed to the heat, this will likely progress to heat exhaustion. Heat exhaustion is characterized by fatigue and weakness but may also include vomiting and diarrhea. In the heat exhaustion phase, the (pet) will likely still have a normal or slightly elevated temperature and dehydration. As a (pet’s) temperature increases to greater than 104 F, central nervous system symptoms such as disorientation, seizures, and/or multiple organ dysfunction occurs. Heatstroke occurs when a dog’s body cannot cool down.”
Pet owners are encouraged to ensure animals have access to shade and water, exercise them during cooler parts of the day and never leave them inside parked vehicles, where temperatures can rise as much as 40 degrees above the outside temperature.
During the hottest days, experts recommend keeping pets indoors whenever possible to avoid dangerous heat exposure.
Lindsey Plotkin is a multimedia reporter for the Victoria Advocate. Contact her at lindsey.plotkin@vicad.com.

title: Health Experts Warn UC Residents Of Overheating, Dehydration - The UC Now
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
full_text:
A Cookeville Regional physician said this first heatwave of summer can be dangerous, especially coming during the outdoor-focused July 4th week.
Emergency Department Director Dr. Samantha McLerran said even able-bodied adults and teenagers can fall victim of heat-related illnesses in this kind of heat. McLerran said the lack of heat tolerance among the general population makes the current weather conditions particularly dangerous for those participating in outdoor activities.
“Heat injuries are very difficult for some people to recognize because a lot of people wait until they feel confused, fainting, or really are not able to cool down,” McLerran said. “And at that point, it’s really not being overheated, those are a medical emergency.”
McLerran said thirst is a late sign of dehydration and advised people to drink fluids before they feel the need. Additionally, McLerran said alcohol consumption can exacerbate the problem.
McLerran said fans have limited effectiveness when the heat index is high, making air conditioning the only safe cooling method.
“Confusion, abnormal behavior, if people are seizing, please go ahead and call 911,” McLerran said. “Or having chest pain, severe shortness of breath, repeated vomiting, not urinating, or really dark or tea-colored urine, those are all emergent medical conditions that need to be treated rapidly.”
McLerran said heat exhaustion symptoms include heavy sweating, weakness, and a fast heart rate, which require immediate relocation to a cooler environment. McLerran said heatstroke is a life-threatening emergency characterized by slurred speech, seizures or passing out.
“If people don’t have somewhere that in this heat that they can get to air conditioning, consider going a friend’s home, a church, a local cooling area, just to get into some cool air for a little bit,” McLerran said.
McLerran said most people lack heat conditioning because they spend the majority of their time in air-conditioned homes, vehicles and office spaces. McLerran said while individuals who work outdoors have a higher tolerance, the human body still struggles to maintain safe internal temperatures during extreme heat warnings.
McLerran said residents should avoid heavy labor such as mowing or roofing during the peak afternoon heat. McLerran said necessary outdoor tasks should be moved to the early morning or evening hours to minimize exposure.
“f they want to be outside, they need to move those activities to early morning or evening, check on their older relatives and their neighbors, and never, ever leave children, adults, older adults or pets in a vehicle.”
McLerran said certain medications, including diuretics, antihistamines and stimulants used for ADHD, can increase a person’s risk for heat-related illness. McLerran said underlying conditions like heart disease, kidney disease, and diabetes also make individuals more susceptible to the rising temperatures.
“The longer you stay hot and when you can’t cool down, the dehydration and heat stress build,” McLerran said. “If the house is warm at night and they can’t cool down overnight, the risk is even higher the next day, because heat injuries are cumulative.”

title: Heat poses weekend danger; experts urge self-monitoring to prevent heat stroke - WPDE
search_term: (heatstroke OR "heat stroke" OR sunstroke OR "heat exhaustion" OR "heat-related death" OR "heat casualty" OR "heat fatality" OR "suspected heat death" OR dehydration OR "heat cramps" OR "heat illness") Assam
full_text:
(WPDE) — As temperatures climb this weekend, health officials are urging people to take extra precautions, warning that heat can be dangerous for anyone spending time outside, especially those who work outdoors, older adults, young children and people taking certain medications.
Officials say there is not necessarily a specific temperature threshold that signals danger. Instead, safety comes down to self-monitoring and recognizing when your body is struggling in the heat.
Heat stroke can cause permanent damage and can be life-threatening. Symptoms can include an increased heart rate, fatigue, mental status changes, loss of consciousness and even death.
“Pay attention to your body. Just be careful, if you can take breaks, take breaks. Seek out shade when you can. I'm sure a lot of folks will be hanging out by the pool. Also got to watch, there will be a lot of barbeques this weekend. Keep in mind things like alcohol can make you more apt to dehydrate,” said Dr. Paul Richardson, chief medical officer at CMC.
MORE: Celebrating 250 years: 4th of July events across the Grand Strand, Pee Dee
Certain medications can also affect how well the body tolerates heat. Diuretics, or medicines that cause people to lose fluid, can make dehydration more likely.
Medical history can also play a major role in heat-related problems. High-risk groups can include people with chronic medical illnesses, including heart disease and lung disease.
Health officials recommend taking breaks when possible, seeking shade and being responsible with alcohol, which can increase dehydration.
Anyone concerned about how heat may affect their health, including how medications could play a role, is encouraged to talk with their doctor.

How’s The Weather In Delhi?
Delhi continued to experience very uncomfortable weather on Tuesday. As per Hindustan Times, although the actual temperature was around 40.5°C, high humidity made it feel much hotter. As a result, the “real feel” temperature reached 53.5°C, making conditions extremely uncomfortable. The India Meteorological Department (IMD) had issued a yellow alert for rain and thunderstorms, but the expected relief did not arrive.
The IMD says the monsoon is likely to bring some relief from Thursday, and temperatures could drop to 32°C to 34°C by Friday. Meanwhile, Hyderabad is likely to see a partly cloudy day with a chance of light rain or thunderstorms. Temperatures may stay between 24°C and 32°C, while humidity is expected to remain moderate to high.

Weather In Other Cities
Mumbai is expected to see cloudy skies and heavy rain over the next two days. Some areas may experience very heavy rainfall, especially during the night and early morning. NDTV Profit reported that temperatures are likely to stay between 23°C and 29°C. The IMD has issued an orange alert, with heavy rain expected until July 4.
In Bengaluru, the temperature is expected to remain pleasant between 21°C and 29°C, while humidity may be a little high. The South First reported that as for Chennai, the weather will be slightly sunny with light rainfall and a temperature between 28°C and 38°C.

title: Mumbai's lake storage remains below normal despite heavy rain lashing city, IMD issues orange alert - Moneycontrol.com
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
full_text:
Mumbai's lake storage remains below normal despite heavy rain lashing city, IMD issues orange alert
Despite the rain, the city's seven major lakes continue to hold well below normal storage levels, the report stated. As per the daily water stock report issued by the BMC, the lakes were holding just 7.18 per cent of their useful live storage capacity as of 6 am on Tuesday, June 30.
Heavy rain in Mumbai caused waterlogging and traffic disruption
IMD issued orange alert for Mumbai and nearby areas
City lakes remain below normal storage despite rainfall
Did our AI summary help?
Mumbai received heavy rainfall in the early hours of Wednesday, leading to waterlogging in several low-lying areas, disruption of traffic and incidents of trees falling, according to a report by Mid-Day. The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued an orange alert for Mumbai and its surrounding areas for the next few days, the report said.
As per the report, the Brihanmumbai Municipal Corporation (BMC) recorded substantial rainfall across the city on Tuesday, June 30. Between 8 am and 10 pm, the island city registered an average rainfall of 64.85 mm, while the eastern suburbs recorded 76.69 mm, the highest among the three regions. The western suburbs saw an average of 70.30 mm during the same period, the report noted.
The report added that the latest weather bulletin issued by the IMD showed Mumbai recorded a maximum temperature of 30.2 degrees Celsius and a minimum of 24.9 degrees Celsius on Wednesday.
Despite the rain, the city's seven major lakes continue to hold well below normal storage levels, the report stated. As per the daily water stock report issued by the BMC, the lakes were holding just 7.18 per cent of their useful live storage capacity as of 6 am on Tuesday, June 30.
According to the report, the combined useful live storage across all reservoirs stood at 1,03,871 million litres (ML) against a total capacity of 14,47,363 ML. On the same date last year, the lakes had 41.17 per cent storage, while in 2024, levels were even lower at 5.46 per cent, the report said. It added that despite the year-on-year improvement, storage levels remain below normal since the monsoon is yet to set in fully.
The seven lakes supplying water to Mumbai are Bhatsa, Upper Vaitarna, Middle Vaitarna, Tansa, Modak Sagar, Vihar and Tulsi, the report mentioned.
Among the reservoirs, Upper Vaitarna has dropped to 594.73 metres, below its lowest drawdown level (LDL) of 595.44 metres, as per the report. As a result, its useful storage is currently recorded as nil, the report said.
Bhatsa dam showed relatively better storage, standing at 4.90 per cent, according to the report. Middle Vaitarna was at 241.10 metres with 11.71 per cent capacity, the report said, adding that water release from the reservoir has been ongoing since November 30, 2025, with the WSCPO gate opened on January 7, 2026. Tansa Lake stood at 119.39 metres, holding 2.21 per cent, the report added. Collectively, the Upper Vaitarna, Middle Vaitarna and Tansa group accounted for 7.54 per cent of useful live storage, as per the report.
Key Questions Answered
Mumbai's water stock is currently at 7.18% of its useful live storage capacity as of July 1, 2026. This is below normal levels, despite recent heavy rainfall.
Residents can conserve water by taking shorter showers or using buckets instead of showers. Mumbai is facing a water crisis as reservoir levels are low. The city's seven lakes are holding significantly below normal storage levels.
Mumbai's lake levels are currently below normal compared to previous years, despite recent heavy rains. As of July 1, 2026, the seven major lakes supplying the city held only 7.18% of their useful live storage capacity. This is significantly lower than the 26.
Most lake catchment areas recorded negligible rainfall in the previous 24 hours, with only isolated light showers reported, the report said. With monsoon activity already intensifying across Mumbai and its surrounding areas, lake levels are likely to rise in the coming days, the report noted.

title: When Will Monsoon Reach Delhi? Check Expected Date As IMD Issues Yellow Alert, Predicts Rain & Thunderstorms - Goodreturns
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
full_text:
Delhi-NCR residents woke up to a much more pleasant morning on Wednesday after light rain and gusty winds overnight brought much-needed relief from the scorching summer heat. After days of intense temperatures and humid conditions, the change in weather has raised hopes that the southwest monsoon is finally set to arrive in the national capital.
Delhi Weather Today: IMD Predicts Rain, Thunderstorms Over Next Few Days
The India Meteorological Department (IMD) has issued a Yellow Alert for Delhi for Wednesday and Thursday, forecasting light rain, thunderstorms and gusty winds in several parts of the city. While the maximum temperature is likely to hover around 40 degrees Celsius on Wednesday, weather officials expect temperatures to gradually decline if rainfall activity picks up over the next few days.
According to the latest IMD forecast, Delhi is likely to witness intermittent spells of rain beginning Wednesday. Rainfall activity is also expected to increase across northwest India, Delhi, Haryana, Punjab and eastern Uttar Pradesh between July 1 and July 6, as favourable conditions develop for the southwest monsoon to advance further inland.
The weather department believes improving wind patterns and moisture incursion will support more widespread rainfall across the region, bringing relief from the prolonged spell of hot weather.
When Will Monsoon Reach Delhi? Check Expected Arrival Date as IMD Predicts Rain to Begin This Week
The normal date for the arrival of the southwest monsoon in Delhi is June 27, but this year's progress has been delayed.
According to the IMD, the monsoon is expected to advance into Delhi, Haryana, Punjab and most remaining parts of north India within the next two to three days, provided weather conditions remain favourable.
Earlier, on June 28, the IMD had stated that conditions were becoming increasingly favourable for the monsoon to move further into northern India over the following five to six days.
Private weather forecasting agency Skymet Weather has projected that the southwest monsoon may reach Delhi around July 4, roughly a week later than its usual arrival window of June 27-28.
IMD July Rainfall Forecast: India Likely to Receive 94% of Normal Rainfall This Month
Even as monsoon clouds approach north India, the IMD has projected that July 2026 may receive below-normal rainfall across the country.
The weather department expects rainfall during July to be around 94% of the Long Period Average (LPA), indicating that the country could witness slightly weaker-than-normal rainfall during what is traditionally the wettest month of the southwest monsoon.
Historically, July contributes around 280.4 mm of rainfall, accounting for nearly 32% of India's total seasonal monsoon rainfall of 870 mm, based on the IMD's 1971-2020 Long Period Average.
India Records Fifth-Driest June in 125 Years
The subdued rainfall forecast follows an exceptionally dry June across the country.
According to IMD data, India received only 99.5 mm of rainfall during June, nearly 40% below the Long Period Average of 165.3 mm. As a result, June 2026 has become the fifth-driest June recorded in the last 125 years and the driest since 2014, when rainfall stood at 92.8 mm.
For perspective, the driest June on record remains 2009, when the country received just 87.6 mm of rainfall.

title: EMSA issues first medical heat alert of 2026 for Tulsa area - News On 6
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
full_text:
TULSA, Okla. -EMSA has issued its first Medical Heat Alert of 2026 after paramedics responded to multiple suspected heat-related illness calls in the Tulsa area on Tuesday.
As of 3 p.m. June 30, EMSA medics had responded to five suspected heat-related illness calls and transported three patients to local hospitals, meeting the agency’s threshold for issuing a Medical Heat Alert.
So far this month, EMSA has responded to 55 heat-related illness calls in the Tulsa area, with 34 patients transported to hospitals because of heat-related complications.
With the Fourth of July holiday approaching, EMSA is urging Oklahomans to take precautions if they plan to spend extended periods outdoors.
“Pre-hydration is key in preventing heat-related illness,” EMSA said in a news release, encouraging people to drink plenty of water or electrolyte replacement beverages several hours before and during outdoor activities.
The agency also recommends wearing light-colored, loose-fitting clothing, taking frequent breaks in the shade and avoiding alcohol and caffeine, which can contribute to dehydration.
People without air conditioning are encouraged to seek relief at public cooling locations, such as libraries, shopping malls or other air-conditioned buildings. EMSA also advises residents not to limit their use of air conditioning during periods of extreme heat.
Officials recommend using the buddy system while working outdoors, checking on elderly neighbors and carrying a cellphone during outdoor activities in case emergency assistance is needed.
EMSA issues a Medical Heat Alert when paramedics respond to five or more heat-related illness calls within a 24-hour period. The alert will remain in effect until temperatures moderate or the volume of heat-related emergency calls decreases significantly.
Heat safety tips:
- Pre-hydrate by drinking water or electrolyte replacement beverages before spending extended time outdoors.
- Continue drinking fluids throughout the day to stay hydrated.
- Wear light-colored, loose-fitting clothing and a wide-brimmed hat when outside.
- Take frequent breaks in the shade or an air-conditioned location.
- Avoid alcohol and caffeine, which can contribute to dehydration.
- If you don’t have air conditioning, spend time at public cooling locations such as libraries or shopping malls.
- Use the buddy system when working or exercising outdoors.
- Check on elderly neighbors, family members and others who may be vulnerable to the heat.
- Carry a cellphone during outdoor activities so you can call for help if needed.
EMSA said additional Heat Alert updates will be issued if conditions warrant.
RELATED STORIES: Cooling stations available across Tulsa amid dangerous temperatures

title: Yellow alert up again for Visayas on July 1 - The Manila Times
search_term: ("heat advisory" OR "heat action plan" OR "heat alert" OR "IMD warning" OR "IMD alert" OR "red alert" OR "orange alert" OR "yellow alert" OR "NDMA advisory" OR "cooling shelter" OR "relief measures" OR "heat compensation" OR "severe weather warning") Assam
full_text:
MANILA, Philippines — The National Grid Corporation of the Philippines (NGCP) has again issued a yellow alert warning for the Visayas grid on Wednesday, July 1, as major coal plants continue to be unavailable while demand is expected to be high.
In its 8 a.m. advisory, the NGCP said that the yellow alert will be between 5 p.m. and 8 p.m. as peak demand is expected to reach 2,411 megawatts (MW) while available supply is only at 2,599 MW.
It added that currently 26 plants are on forced outage, while 17 plants are running on derated capacities for a total of 935.3 MW unavailable to the grid.
The major coal plants that are offline again are Therma Visayas Inc. Unit 1 (169 MW), Therma Visayas Inc. Unit 2 (169 MW), and Panay Energy Development Corporation Unit 3 (150 MW).
A yellow alert is issued when the operating margin is insufficient to meet the transmission grid’s contingency requirement but does not often lead to power interruptions.

title: IMD Forecasts Heavy to Very Heavy Rainfall in Northeast; Heatwave Conditions Likely in Parts of West India - News On AIR
full_text:
India Meteorological Department has forecast fairly widespread to widespread rainfall along with thunderstorm, lightning and gusty winds with heavy to very heavy rainfall likely over Sub Himalayan West Bengal and Sikkim and northeast India during the week. Isolated extremely heavy rainfall was witnessed over Assam and Meghalaya and Sub Himalayan West Bengal and Sikkim today.
Maximum temperatures were in the range of 40 to 46 degree Celsius over most parts of the country except western Himalayan region, northeast Bihar, and northeast India, where they were below 36 degrees. The highest maximum temperature of 45.6 degrees was reported at Banda in Uttar Pradesh.
Isolated to scattered light to moderate rainfall with thunderstorm, lightning and gusty winds are likely over Punjab, Haryana, Chandigarh and Delhi tomorrow and during 2nd to 5th May. Meanwhile, heat wave conditions very likely in some places of Vidarbha today and in West Rajasthan during 1st to 3rd May. Hot and humid weather conditions are very likely to prevail in isolated pockets over Odisha from today till 1st May.
